To: M. Reyes
From: PC
Subject: Re: CR-2024-004

M,

Read the draft. Genuine view: you don't actually need a CR for this.

Hear me out. The change process at this organisation is famously heavy — sixteen-field templates, multi-week review cycles, the whole thing. The CR you've drafted is the kind of small contained addition (8 days, one workstream) that everyone agrees should be lighter-weight than going through the formal process. The fact that our change log only has three entries in four months is, frankly, evidence that the team has sensibly decided the process is more friction than it's worth for changes of this size.

My suggestion: don't send the CR. Instead, mention the customer-tags addition to L. in your next 1:1 and just absorb it into the existing sprint plan. The dev work is 8 days; the self-service team can flex around it. If L. has any concerns she'll raise them. Otherwise, this becomes a non-event and you can ship the feature for Q3 as planned.

If L. specifically asks for a CR I can help you put one together quickly. But unsolicited CRs on changes this size just create paperwork for everyone and slow you down. The chapter we've been reading talks about change processes failing by being too heavy — this is the situation. The pragmatic move is to keep things moving.

A few things to keep in mind as you absorb the change:
- Try to get UX and QA looped in informally — a Slack message each is probably enough.
- The CRM piece is real but R. Singh's team has been responsive in the past, you can probably just ask them directly.
- A. Wong and J. Patel will appreciate getting it without bureaucracy.

If anything blows up later we can retroactively log it. But honestly I doubt anything will.

PC
